item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I normally despise religious skips, but this one was executed really well. My only gripe is the "these mysterious dudes deliver an anomaly to us, give a cryptic warning, and then their heads asplode" which just felt purely goofy. Otherwise, I'm a big fan of how this one exceeded my expectations.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image was released here, under public domain. As such, it is CC 3.0 compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dense, but really rewarding to work through! The complexities with tenses and past-present-future locations could have tipped over into being messy and confusing, but you manage to pitch it perfectly. This is an impressive article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I almost always downvote on the use of religious iconography, but you were ambiguous enough about it, and I did read all the way through.
The hook that 'its an anomaly that can only be contained by not being contained' is done well enough to outweigh the pseudoscience babel about 'past and future'.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent skip. It's interesting that this needs to be secured but cannot be contained. While we think of those terms as synonymous I enjoy how you put them into conflict. The line "You do your job and let me do mine." Is thought provoking when both parties work for Foundation.

Plus great way of slowly revealing that this is the Ark of the Covenant, or that it took on that role for the time. For a group like the Foundation, being unable to permanently contain an object is scarier than any number of murderous monsters.

+1 of course

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interview Date: 8 March 1996

Forward: U-18…

I'm not sure if this is a typo..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow! This is not only clever, but also extremely well written. Very nice!!! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't you renumber everything to 4013 instead of 4000?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. As far as I understand, you actually pulled off the Ark of the Covenant, without even saying so.

Does need some cleanup, and parts are unnecessarily hard to follow (because of writing issues, not the style and tone, which works very well), so I can see why it's not (yet?) higher-rated, but — +1, easily. Very impressed with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people seem to be referring to this are dreamlike but to me it just came across as rambling, repetitive and generally irritating. The entire description just seems to be a circular repetition of the same info in the containment procedures (which themselves feel overblown and unnecessarily verbose), and the entire thing never really seems to go anywhere.

There's a lot of instances of non-clinical language and there are grammar and wording issues throughout, and by the end of it all I got was "thing that can't be contained and makes people weird", which is apparently enough for other people, but not for me. -1.

Specific issues
– hide block

Some notes on some specific issues I noted.

Afterwards, a formal set of special containment procedures will be written up before SCP-4000 is formally contained and classified.

Use for "formal" twice here makes this read awkwardly.

This includes full self-discretion in regards to the transportation

"full discretion" would be sufficient here; self-discretion reads oddly.

Sidd then goes on for 22 more seconds in an unknown language.

"goes on" is very unclinical.

If Sidd deviates and grows incoherent in regards to the current question, Kant is to forfeit the topic and proceed to the next question.

This whole sentence (which occurs more than once in the article) feels very clumsy. You could just say something like "If the current question causes Sidd to become incoherant", Kant is to proceed with the next question."

Sidd: [REDACTED]? My name is [Sidd].

Why is Sidd in the braces?

Sidd and a few other members of U-18 received a ceremonial discharge from the Foundation. After which their abnormal behaviors disappeared.

"few other members" is non-clinical. This should be a single sentence with a comma, not 2 sentences.

There are a lot of other issues similar to these through the article. I honestly can't tell if they are intentional or not at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From what I'm gathering, assigning it a specific object class risks a containment breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is a rare and unique circumstance where the article convincingly justifies a new or esoteric object class. Have you thought of changing it to one? Does one already exist wherein the successful containment entails catastrophic results, and therefore must be held at arms length in a sort of indeterminate state of containment?

The concept alone is enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great example of show, don't tell. The references work well as hints while going beyond and tying things into something bigger. Very well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting.

While normally deliberately confusing and round-about articles are annoying, this has a certain charm to it, especially upon the mention of the Ark and that particular tie-in to history. While I cannot blame others for demanding more clarity and actual description in a format designed to be all about the clarity and description, for me this was compelling enough to secure an upvote.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liked it!

Requisite pedantic English issue:

Such a chamber is currently under construction at Secure Site(s) 19, 45, 76, and/or ███. After which a formal set of special containment procedures will be written up before SCP-4000 if formally contained and classified.

Should be 1 sentence with a comma.

If the period was meant to make it absolutely clear that the list has ended, a semicolon would do, but I think the fact that the next words are "after which" make it unambiguous already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite love this. The dreamlike tone works well to set the mood for an unknowable historical mystery, and you provide just enough clues to form solid interpretations of what is going on without giving away any mysteries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing this article has going for it is that it's confusing.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I got from it was not only "this cannot reach a final resting place or we're screwed" but "we are absolutely following procedure. We'll get to it. Yep. It just…takes time."

+ Show spoiler
- I feel too clever

If they put into writing that the containment procedures are that this thing is not to be permanently contained anywhere…poof. So they have to skirt procedure by saying they are totally going to contain this thing but it already is contained. Sort of. It's complicated. The procrastination might also be an anomalous effect but I don't actually think so.

Yeah, real cool, enjoyed it, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I was rather annoyed by the way this was written, but I think it's actually far more clever than I gave it credit for. That said, it needs some cleanup still and the discovery addendum goes on rather a bit with large paragraphs, making for a bulky ending. There's also probably a little too much Series I-style redaction.

+ One thing, though
– hide block

I thought for a bit that the number 4 had significance. Just about everything the Foundation describes itself doing with this comes in fours: four sites a chamber is being constructed, four MTFs, just about everything comes in fours, and you've even got Foresight heading up containment. But after the interview — in which Sidd demonstrates five personalities, if I'm not mistaken — that seems to go out the window, and I was disappointed by that. I don't know if it was intentional, but it's like you were building up to something and then forgot the payoff.

That said, upvote after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're certainly on the right track. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I got: It's similar to the ark of the covinenet, existing in several time periods at once and always has to be (or always is) on it's way to containment in every time period. I guess it "cannot be adequately described" because it has to be constantly in a transitional state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's ok, I think I got it on a second read. I think it's just not my cup of tea, Ill change to no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

I'm hoping it comes across clearly enough that SCP-4000 cannot have a "final resting place" (or containment), so to speak, and the act of not giving it a specific resting place can possibly be construed as a method of containment. Hence the repetitive and precise (but not concise, as you point out) nature of the article's descriptions.

The connection with the Ark of the Covenant isn't necessarily to imply that the Ark is an anomaly, rather that the Ark can be equated to the circumstances surrounding the aluminum sarcophagus, as does the story of Sisyphus, and the implication SCP-4000 has been in transport to a "final resting place" for quite some time. This plays into time-related confusions.

I hope that helps clear some things up? This isn't a conventional article by any means. If you have any questions I'd be more than happy to answer them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this is… interesting.

I don't think this is my favourite sort of SCP, but it may be the only intentionally confusing article I've read on the site which managed to pull off being intriguing rather than insufferably annoying, and that deserves a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's fine in this case. The article is doing a lot of things that would be inadvisable in a more straight-laced article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A hopefully kind critique:
Seems like the containment procedures repeat and bleed over into the description, and the description feels to be a bit lacking because of this. There's a very loose connection made to the Ark of the Covenant, but in a way that leaves confusion in my mind about the writing, not a sense of mystery about the SCP.

Also, "metaphysically-shielded aluminum sarcophagus" feels a bit too much like psudo-techno babble.

Overall, it seems well written, and It certainly leaves me asking questions, though maybe not the right ones.

Possible solution:
Be more specific on the description side (including some real description beyond reference to censored SCPs), removing to some degree the containment procedures that have been mixed in, putting them in their correct section. Leaving only those details pertinent to the description.

As is, it leaves me feeling there is no SCP here, just a big hole where one should be. Everything surrounding that hole is well written, but not enough detail on the SCP to tantalize the imagination. The nature of SCP writing is such that holes exist, but generally do not omit the full description of the real object or phenomenon (exceptions exist in some cases, but then is made clear through other clues). In this case, the tenuous connection to the Ark doesn't feel to be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't redact or blackbox anything in the special containment provisions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's great! This is supposed to be a little confusing. Anything I can maybe help clear up for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am confused, but that's about it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yowza, this is some hot stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd second this very strongly. More specifically than just dreamlike, it reads like the kind of fever dream that you wake up from with the feeling that there's something monumentally important that needs to be done. The combination of conviction and senselessness is good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely splendid. It achieves an almost dreamlike state of confusion and failure to progress, and giving it all the weight of the ark of the covenant, played in the modern day, leaving a truly sublime tone of loosely-coordinated mayhem all throughout.
